core_concept: Chemical Reactions and Matter: Atoms, Bonds, Reactions
facts: An atom's chemical identity depends on the number of protons in its nucleus; for example, any atom with 6 protons is carbon, and any atom with 8 protons is oxygen.

In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons, so both atoms are attracted to the same electrons; in an ionic bond, one atom transfers electrons to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other.
During a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged into new molecules, but no atoms are created or destroyed; the total number of each type of atom remains the same before and after.
A molecule is a neutral group of two or more atoms held together by covalent bonds; for example, a water molecule (H2O) has two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom bonded covalently.
Protons carry a positive electric charge and electrons carry a negative charge; opposite charges attract, which is why electrons are held near the nucleus and why atoms can bond.
Chemical bonds are not solid connectors but electrical attractions; for example, a covalent bond exists because the nuclei of both atoms are attracted to the same shared electrons.
misconceptions: Students believe that atoms are solid, indivisible spheres with no internal structure, not realizing they contain protons and electrons.
Students believe that when a substance burns, the atoms are destroyed and new atoms appear; for example, they think the ash weighs less because some atoms vanished.
Students think that in every chemical bond, electrons are shared equally between atoms; they do not recognize that in ionic bonds electrons are transferred, not shared.
